coredata.dc:title: Interface and doping in carbon dots influence charge transfer and transport
coredata.dc:creator: Mishra, Leepsa
Behera, Ranjan Kumar
Mondal, Sankalan
Kumar, Satish
Panigrahi, Aradhana
Sarangi, Manas Kumar
coredata.dc:description:
Nanoscale carbon-based materials have the potential for large-scale optoelectronic and biomedical applications due to their tunable photo-physics. Here, we explore the impact of doping and the role of intervening media on both electron transfer and transport from carbon dots (CD) to an acceptor menadione (MD). In a binary mixture compared to undoped-CD (UCD), the amine-based nitrogen-doped-CD (ACD) reflects significant changes in its photo-physics like excitation-independent fluorescence, enhanced lifetime, along with a distinct drop in electron transfer rate (k
ET ) to MD. With micro-emulsion, k
ET from UCD to MD is enhanced while a substantial reduction is observed for ACDs, emphasizing the critical role of doping, surface states, encapsulation, and interfaces of CTAB micelles. The impact of doping and Coulombic interaction at interfaces on the charge transport phenomena with redox-active MD is further investigated by current-sensing atomic force microscopy. For similar bias-voltage, ACD reflects a higher conductance than UCD, and the conductance is enhanced in the presence of CTAB micelles. Moreover, at a low voltage regime, the current-voltage curve shows a linear growth, while at higher voltage, Fowler-Nordheim (F–N) tunneling is observed. The presence of MD, however, leads to an abrupt rise in conductivity resulting in a transition from F–N to direct tunneling.

In (c), the black dotted sharp decay represents the instrument response function (IRF). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Steady-state fluorescence quenching (λex = 380 nm) (a, c) and time-resolved lifetime decay (b, d) of UCDs (0.02 mg/ml) with the gradual increment of the concentration of MD (0–2 mM) in H2O–EtOH mixture (a, b) and CTAB (20 mM) (c, d) with λex = 377 nm and emission at their corresponding emission peaks. The emission spectra of UCDs (without MD) in the H2O–EtOH mixture and CTAB medium are in custom wine and cyan colours, respectively. Here we are gradually adding the concentration as 0.2 mM (green), 0.4 mM (red), 0.6 mM (olive), 0.8 mM (custom brown), 1 mM (dark yellow), 1.2 mM (violet), 1.4 mM (pink), 1.6 mM (navy), 2 mM (dark cyan) of MD for both the medium. head.ce:title: Interface and doping in carbon dots influence charge transfer and transport

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Bihar, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Bihar, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Nanoscale carbon-based materials have the potential for large-scale optoelectronic and biomedical applications due to their tunable photo-physics. Here, we explore the impact of doping and the role of intervening media on both electron transfer and transport from carbon dots (CD) to an acceptor menadione (MD). In a binary mixture compared to undoped-CD (UCD), the amine-based nitrogen-doped-CD (ACD) reflects significant changes in its photo-physics like excitation-independent fluorescence, enhanced lifetime, along with a distinct drop in electron transfer rate (k
ET ) to MD. With micro-emulsion, k
ET from UCD to MD is enhanced while a substantial reduction is observed for ACDs, emphasizing the critical role of doping, surface states, encapsulation, and interfaces of CTAB micelles. The impact of doping and Coulombic interaction at interfaces on the charge transport phenomena with redox-active MD is further investigated by current-sensing atomic force microscopy. For similar bias-voltage, ACD reflects a higher conductance than UCD, and the conductance is enhanced in the presence of CTAB micelles. Moreover, at a low voltage regime, the current-voltage curve shows a linear growth, while at higher voltage, Fowler-Nordheim (F–N) tunneling is observed. The presence of MD, however, leads to an abrupt rise in conductivity resulting in a transition from F–N to direct tunneling.

ce:section.ce:para: While the charge transfer and transport across organic molecules have been studied extensively, the same for low dimensional quantum dot interface needs more exploration as it holds promise for large scale applications in photonics, electronics, catalysis, bio-imaging, etc. Low dimensional semiconductor materials possess anomalous optoelectronic properties and are used for efficient electro and photocatalytic activity, often involving interfacial charge transfer processes [9–11]. Ahmed et al. have tuned the interfacial ET rates from perovskite nanocrystals to tetracyanoethylene by varying the shapes of the nanocrystals [12]. With fluorescence quenching and transient absorption, they have reported that the spherical nanocrystals have higher ET efficiency than the platelets and the cubes. With benzoquinone as an electron acceptor and phenothiazine as a hole acceptor, Wu et al. have determined both the electron and hole transfer and their recombination kinetics at the interfaces of CsPbBr3 QDs, which are critical parameters for their device based applications [13]. Based on the ET efficacy of different sized CdSe/ZnS QDs to a neurotransmitter molecule dopamine, Clarke et al. have fluorescent-labelled the redox-sensitive pattern inside live cells [14]. Within the reducing environment, they have imaged the cell periphery and lysosomes, while in oxidizing conditions, they are able to label the perinuclear zone and mitochondria. Medintz et al. have observed a similar ET-based quenching of QD photoluminescence in QD-dopamine-peptide bioconjugates for in vitro and intracellular pH-sensing [15]. Moreover, Gudli et al. have found that the covalent linkage between carbon nanodots as electron D and an electron A can effectively promote the strong electronic interaction, which leads to charge separation [16,17]. Besides, Reisner et al. have demonstrated CDs as photosensitizers for light-driven catalysis with redox enzymes exploiting the role of surface charge of CDs utilizing a fumarate reductase or a hydrogenase [18].

Herein we use a series of spectroscopic and conducting measurements to emphasize the role of doping, nature of interface on the charge migration from nanoscale CDs to a molecular acceptor vitamin K3 menadione (MD). CDs are easy to prepare, photo-stable, bio-compatible, non-toxic, water-soluble with several promising applications in bio-imaging, sensing, solar cells, LEDs, etc. Redox modifications on citric acid-based CDs have shown severe disruption in their optical and electrical conductance when treated with reducing or oxidizing agents [19]. Due to surface defect trap states, multiple chromophoric units, presence of dangling bonds, etc., UCDs often exhibit excitation dependent photoluminescence [20,21]; however, nitrogen-doped CDs have been reported to have excitation independent photoluminescence, with tunable optical-electronic properties [22]. Quinones and their derivatives are redox-active and abundant in all life forms present in nature. They facilitate charge transfer in critical biochemical processes like photosynthesis, respiration, cellular homeostasis, etc. [23–26]. Through sequential acceptance of one electron (unstable semi-quinone) and two electrons, quinones (stable form) get converted to the parent quinol [27] (Scheme 1 ).

Single molecular conductance studies on the redox properties of the quinones have shown their ability to act as molecular switches by means of electrochemical gating, as well as in many other optoelectronic devices like rectifiers, transistors, diodes, etc. With the help of scanning tunneling microscopy break junction technique, N. Darwish et al. have shown that anthraquinone moiety can electrochemically and reversibly switch in situ between its high-conducting reduced form and low-conducting oxidized form [28]. They have attributed an order magnitude of on/off ratio to the destructive quantum interference of the anthraquinone molecules. Here, we highlight the investigation on the role of doping, encapsulation, nature of the interface, and the intervening media on charge transfer of CDs to MD in homogeneous and bio-mimicking micellar environments. The charged interfaces of CDs and the non-covalent interactions like hydrogen bonding, ion pair, and Columbic interactions may be detrimental to the charge transfer processes. We employ various spectroscopic and conducting measurements across both undoped and nitrogen-doped CDs to measure alteration in the charge transfer kinetics at the interface with encapsulated MD inside CTAB micelles and charge transport across a single metal-CD-metal junction.

Materials: The compounds MD (98%), CTAB (≥99%), ethylenediamine (EDA) (≥99%) are purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used as received. The citric acid (≥99.5%, anhydrous) is purchased from Merck, which is used for the synthesis of CDs. Syringe filters are also purchased from Merck. Milli-Q (MQ) water is used for the whole preparation. Highly Oriented Pyrolytic Graphite (HOPG) of size 1 cm × 1 cm is purchased from SPI supplies. All the experiments are performed at room temperature in ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: We have used the same methods of preparation of CDs, as followed in the previous report by Bera et al. [29]. Preparation of UCDs
ce:section.ce:para: 1 gm of citric acid is dissolved in 10 ml of MQ water and pyrolyzed for 4 h at 150 °C (hot plate temperature above 250 °C). A yellow-colored solution is prepared, which ensures the formation of UCDs. Then it is lyophilized to get dried CDs and stored for future use. Preparation of ACDs: ACDs are synthesized in a similar way to CDs and are obtained by pyrolyzing the citric acid in water for the first 4 h and then in EDA for the next 1 h. A dark brown colored jelly-like solution is formed, confirming the formation of ACDs, and then filtered by using syringe filter with 0.2 μm pore size. The details of the preparation and purification are provided elsewhere [19].

Steady-state absorption spectra and photoluminescence spectra are recorded by JASCO V-770 absorption spectrophotometer and fluoromax-4 spectro-fluorimeter, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the CDs are recorded using a PerkinElmer spectrum 400 spectrometer in ATR mode. Quartz cuvettes of 10 mm path length are used for all the spectroscopic experiments. We have used 0.02 mg/ml of UCDs and 0.02 mg/ml of ACDs for the spectroscopic experimentation at pH 7. As MD is not soluble in water, we have made the stock solution of 1 mM in water and ethanol (1:1) mixture, which has been used for further experimentation. For all the measurements in confined media, we have kept the concentration of CTAB micelles fixed at 20 mM. Here, for the preparation of 20 mM of CTAB micelles, 72.89 mg of CTAB (Sigma, ≥99%) is added in 10 ml of Milli-Q water. Zeta potential measurements of UCDs and ACDs are conducted by using Litesizer 500 of Anton Paar company at room temperature with irradiation from a 660 nm laser.

Time-resolved emission spectra are detected by using a picosecond pulsed diode laser-based Horiba TCSPC spectrometer and MCP-PMT as a detector. The data are fitted to the multi-exponential function

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of rate and efficiency of ET
ce:section.ce:para:
From the average lifetime values of UCDs in the absence and presence of MD, the rate of quenching is calculated using the Stern-Volmer (SV) plot given by

TEM measurements are performed using aFEI, Tecnai G2 F30, S-Twin microscope operating at 300 kV equipped with a GATAN Orius SC1000B CCD camera. The samples prepared in water are drop-casted on 300-mesh carbon-coated copper grids. Then to dry it, the grids are kept in a vacuum condition till the complete drying up of the samples. Then, the grids are used for TEM measurements, and ImageJ software is used for the analysis.

Topography images are taken in the non-contact mode (model: Agilent Technologies 5500). CS-AFM measurements are done in contact mode using a Pt-coated cantilever in ambient conditions. I–V curves are measured in identical conditions at room temperature. The CDs are taken first in the H2O–EtOH mixture and then in CTAB and spin-coated on HOPG substrate at a speed of 1000 rpm for 1 min individually. Then, 1 mM MD is added to the CDs solutions both in H2O–EtOH mixture or in CTAB, and then spin-coated on HOPG’s. (Few layers of HOPG are peeled up from the specimen bought from the vendor). The bias voltage is swept from −2.0 V to +2.0 V, whereas there is no alteration shown in the I–V curves in the reverse sweep from +2 V to −2 V WSxM 5.0 developer 8.3 software is used to process the AFM images, and Origin 8.5 version software is used for plotting all the graphs. so to find out the type of tunneling, we have fitted the results with F–N equation for the samples, which is given as [30].

Sample preparation for AFM measurement: Both the CDs solutions are deposited on highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG) by spin-coating method. Few layers of HOPG are piled out by scotch tape and glued on a glass slide using double-sided tape. The substrate is spin-coated with the CDs solutions at 1000 rpm for 60 s. All procedures for film preparation are conducted at room temperature. The spin-coated CDs on the HOPG substrate are used for the study of AFM topography in non-contact mode using SSS-NCH silicon tips and for CS-AFM in contact mode using Pt coated cantilever.

The resistances obtained in Table 1 are used to calculate the change in resistance. The percentage of the ratio of the difference in the resistance of CDs in the absence (R0) and presence (R) of MD to the resistance of CDs in the absence of MD is the change in resistance, which is given by ΔR/R0%.

The morphology and size of both the CDs are examined by TEM and AFM (Fig. 1 - a to f). TEM images (Fig. 1-a, b) and the size distribution (Fig. 1-e, f) suggest that both UCDs and ACDs are spherical with the average diameters of ∼5 nm and ∼4 nm, respectively, which are in good agreement with the reported literature [31]. Fig. 1-c, d give the AFM images of these CDs, which show the spherical shapes CDs with height 3–4 nm (inset). FTIR spectroscopic measurements for UCDs show strong absorption bands at 1635 cm−1 (CO stretching) and 3328 cm−1 (O–H stretching vibrations) indicating the presence of carboxylic acid. Similar measurements on ACDs shows strong absorption bands at 1645 cm−1 (CO stretching) and 1284 cm−1 (C–N stretching) indicative of the presence of amide group, proving successful nitrogen-doping in ACDs. For the UCD molecules, the carbonyl group stretching frequency at 1635 cm−1 indicates the presence of carboxylate surface states, which show quite good congruence with the previous reports [32,33].

The absorption spectra, as shown in Fig. 2 (a), display higher absorbance for UCDs and ACDs at the blue-end in the water media due to π-π∗ transitions of aromatic domains [34]. For both the cases, the absorbance appears at 245 nm and 350 nm, attributed to π-π∗ transition (CC) and n-π∗ transition (CO), respectively [34]. However, in the confined CTAB micellar media, the peak around 350 nm becomes more prominent in both UCDs and ACDs, while the peaks at the blue end are significantly reduced, indicating a decrease in the π-π∗ transition. This is due to the cut-off wavelength of CTAB at the extreme UV region [35,36]. Fig. 2 (b) and the inset of Fig. 2 (a) show the fluorescence spectra of UCDs and ACDs in water and CTAB micelles. It is noticeable that the micellar environment results in a blue shift in the emission maxima of UCDs from 470 nm in water to 463 nm in CTAB, indicating the influence of the change in the polar environment in the vicinity of micellar interfaces. However, this shift is very minimal for ACDs at 455 nm, with limited alteration in its emissive behavior due to restricted interaction at the interface of the CTAB micelles. Moreover, ACDs have emission maxima at 455 nm, which is independent of the excitation wavelength, while red-shifted UCDs emission at 470 nm in water depends on its choice of excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. S1 (a) and (b). This highlights the presence and the importance of various surface states in the UCDs molecules, which leads to its excitation dependency [20,21]. Along with the steady-state measurement, the time-resolved fluorescence decay of UCDs and ACDs are also investigated in both the water and CTAB as framed in Fig. 1 (c). As is evident from the decay profiles, the nano-confined medium brings a distinct rise in the average lifetime of UCDs from 3.82 ns to 4.15 ns, while a negligible change in the lifetime of ACDs (14.70 ns in H2O–EtOH mixture and 14.50 ns in CTAB). These results are in line with the steady-state measurements (Fig. 2 (a, b)) and highlight the role of the surface states in the emissive behavior of UCDs and ACDs in these two media. In H2O–EtOH media, due to the intermolecular hydrogen bonding with the solvent molecules, which gets strengthened in the excited state, the rate of internal conversion will increase. This causes an increase in the non-radiative decay pathway and hence decreases the fluorescence lifetime [37–39]. The alteration in the radiative and non-radiative pathways results in a drop in the value of the average lifetime in UCDs (3.82 ns). In comparison, the cationic CTAB micelles possess electrostatic interaction with UCDs causing an increase in the lifetime to 4.15 ns. On the contrary, in ACDs, there is no change in lifetime value in the CTAB medium. One can note that the surface charge is playing a crucial role in such photo-physical processes. Due to this reason, we have measured the zeta potential of UCDs and ACDs to get the idea about their surface charges, as shown in Fig. S2. The mean zeta potential values for UCDs and ACDs are found to be −8.0 ± 0.4 mV and 3.2 ± 0.4 mV, respectively, which are in congruence with previous reports [40,41], and suggest that UCDs have negatively, and ACDs have positively charged surface. Hence, the significant lifetime change in case of UCDs and negligible change in ACDs can be assigned to the electrostatic interaction of negatively charged UCDs with the cationic micelle CTAB results is more as compared to the positively charged ACDs with cationic CTAB, which is well-explained later. The lifetime decays are fitted with three-exponential functions (Equation (1)), and the fitting parameters with the average lifetime (Equation (2)) are provided in Table ST1. UCDs have sp2-carbon core, and the surface is often functionalized with different groups like CO, C–O, –COOH groups [42]. These dangling bonds facilitate the non-radiative pathways by trapping the excitons and act as trapping sites. The N- doping in ACDs diminishes the number of trapping sites and hence, facilitating the radiative pathways. This results in the abrupt rise in the excited state lifetime to 14.70 ns in ACDs [19,22]. Naccache et al. have shown that the longer lifetime and higher quantum yield in ACDs are caused by the emergence of new surface states that help in trapping the electrons facilitating the radiative recombination [43]. To investigate the photo-physics of the CDs in film, we have performed a comparative study of emission and excitation spectra of UCDs in both water and thin-film as shown in Fig. S1 (c). The spectral behavior shows similar features for the both the excitation and emission characteristics of UCDs in the solution and thin films. We considered solutions-based measurements for the spectroscopic studies as it is easy to prepare and operate and followed thin-film based measurements for its optoelectronics characterization.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the quenching of fluorescence intensity of UCDs (custom wine) with the gradual increase in the concentration of MD from 0 to 2 mM in the H2O–EtOH mixture. However, the time-resolved lifetime decays (Fig. 3 (b) and Table ST2) show a negligible variation in the average lifetime of UCDs from 3.82 ns to 3.72 ns in the H2O–EtOH mixture. On the other hand, a very consistent quenching of UCDs is observed in CTAB in the presence of MD (Fig. 3 (c)). Moreover, we observe a gradual drop in the lifetime of UCDs from 4.15 ns to 2.46 ns in the CTAB medium with the increase in MD concentration from Fig. 3 (d) and Table ST2. This shows intriguing fluorescence quenching due to charge transfer from UCDs to MD only at the cationic micellar interface of CTAB. The black line signifies the non-fluorescent behavior of MD in both the media. It has been reported that the micro-confinement of the probe molecules in the hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions of the micelles, along with the charges of the micellar head groups, greatly influence fluorescence quenching rates [44–46]. From Fig. S3, it is clear that the absorption of UCDs at 340 nm increases with the increase in MD concentration, which is because of the absorption of MD in the same range as shown in the insets. Here, the noteworthy point is that Fig. 3 (a) shows a drop in the fluorescence intensity in the presence of MD without any alteration in lifetime value, indicative of a ground state interaction between the two molecules.

As shown in Fig. 4
(a, b), the steady-state and time-resolved measurements of ACDs elucidates a drop in the fluorescence intensity as well as lifetime values with the addition of MD in H2O–EtOH solution. The average fluorescence lifetime of ACDs is significantly changed from 14.70 ns to 12.53 ns by MD (0.5 mM) addition in the H2O–EtOH mixture as tabulated in Table ST3 , which reveals the excited state collisional quenching of ACDs by MD in H2O–EtOH medium. Contrastingly, in the CTAB medium (Fig. 4 (c, d)), there is negligible quenching of ACDs as unveiled from steady-state as well as time-resolved fluorescent decay profiles with the lifetime changing from 14.50 ns to 14.20 ns after the addition of MD. The above observations reveal that dynamic quenching of UCDs by MD is ensuing in CTAB only, while the conflicting results are found in the case of ACDs, where there is negligible quenching. It is important to note that in the case of UCDs in the H2O–EtOH mixture, the quenching is static with no variation in the lifetime value, which may be due to ground state complex formation. To verify it, we have observed absorption spectra (Fig. S3). However, we have failed to see any shift in the absorption peak or emergence of any new peak here. Hence, to go deep into the quenching mechanism, we have observed excitation spectra, as shown in Fig. S4, for all the four systems at their corresponding emission maxima. As shown here, the excitation spectra for UCDs have two peaks at ⁓355 nm and ⁓260 nm, which indicates that there are two types of excitation energy trapped at the surface of CDs [22]. However, with the addition of MD, the excitation peak at 355 nm is shifting to 381 nm, along with a rise in a new peak at 295 nm with the addition of MD (Fig. S4 (a)). This indicates the formation of the ground-state complex between UCDs and MD in the H2O–EtOH mixture. More interestingly, similar results are also noticed in CTAB (Fig. S4 (b)). However, in this case, the average lifetime is again dropping by the gradual addition of MD. This suggests a combination of both static and dynamic quenching in UCDs by MD when they are confined in micelles, similar to an earlier report [47].

Bera et al. have reported the charge transfer between MD and Ruthenium doped CDs, where it is shown that MD can be used as an electron acceptor [29]. However, the process depends on the distance between the D and A as they have used a linker that can bind the Ruthenium doped CDs to the quencher, MD. Similarly, in our case, the distance between the CDs and MD is tuned by the environmental conditions (in the H2O–EtOH mixture and CTAB medium). For the detection of μM levels of iodine in water media, Zhang et al. have emphasized the role of strong electrostatic interactions of I− with the positive interfaces of the nitrogen-doped CDs [41]. Consequently, the UCDs with the negatively charged surface can be more interactive to MD at the cationic interface of the CTAB medium [40]. As a result, UCDs and MD come closer to the cationic interface, which assists in a more predominant interaction between the two (Scheme 2 (a)). On the contrary, the positive charge surface of the ACDs cannot interact efficiently with MD due to the electrostatic force created at the CTAB interface [46]. Our hypothesis on this charge transfer mechanism in both the systems is reinforced by Scheme 2 and Scheme S1. Stabilization of the intermediates formed in an ET process through hydrogen bonding and ion-pair interactions leads to a decrease in the activation energy of the ET process and hence increase in the kinetics of the ET. Further, such stabilization also leads to the shifting of the equilibrium to the reduced states compared to the states were such stabilization are absent [49–51]. As shown in Scheme S1 (a), the carboxyl group in UCDs equilibrates into carboxylate form producing protons in H2O–EtOH mixture. When photo-excited, the carboxylate form of UCDs, due to the negative charge, can donate electrons through PET to the MD molecules resulting in the formation of the quinolate and the decarboxylation product Scheme S1 (b) [52–55]. However, in the CTAB medium, the carboxylate form in UCDs may form strong ion pair interaction with the CTAB molecule, stabilizing the carboxylate group (Scheme S1 (c)). This will shift the equilibria more to the carboxylate form, resulting in higher concentration of the carboxylate anion. Further, this shifting of the equilibrium due to greater stability of the carboxylate form (conferred through ion-pair interaction), may result in changing to a better electron donor, carboxylate. Another point that may be envisioned is the fact that as stated above in the UCD molecules, in H2O–EtOH medium, intermolecular hydrogen bonding between the H2O–EtOH medium and the UCD molecules are present. Now, it is well reported in the literature, hydrogen bonding interaction influences the ET in quinone molecules [56–58]. A similar type of solvent assisted hydrogen bond brings the D and A together, enhancing the ET process. However, in cationic CTAB micelles, the electrostatic ion-pair interaction between the surface states of the UCD and the micelle brings D and A in close proximity resulting in the ET process. This will result in a higher ET rate and hence higher fluorescence quenching in UCD molecules. Furthermore, in ACDs, the positively charged NH3 + groups in ACDs may be dissociated into NH2 groups with concomitant release of protons (Scheme S1 (d)). The radical cation formed in ACDs will be unstable in the CTAB medium due to a strong electrostatic repulsion of the radical cation with the CTAB molecules (Scheme 2 (b)). This results in a very low ET rate for the ACD molecules in the CTAB media as compared to that in the H2O–EtOH medium ( Scheme S1 (e)). Such a type of reaction has also been reported by Butkevich et al. while showing oxidative photo-bluing pathways for fluorescent dyes with (di)alkylamino auxochromic groups [59]. Similar observation is revealed by the previous report, which shows charge transfer from pyranine molecule to acetate in cyclodextrin nanocavity [60]. In this report, we observe that the interfacial charges of the micelles, along with confinement-induced optimal distancing between D and A, play a pivotal role in the charge transfer mechanism, which is reinforced by many reports [61,62].

Though the interfacial charge transfer mechanism has been observed optically using spectroscopic technique, we have also observed this process electrically by using CSAFM. It is one of the most powerful techniques in molecular electronics to study the electrical properties, which can also provide information about the topography, surface conductivity, dopant profiling, barrier height, and local electronic states of the quantum-confined nanoscale materials [63,64]. In this process, there is an electrical contact with its conducting tip to the surface of CDs, which leads to the formation of a metal−CD−metal junction. The alteration in current and voltage (I–V) characteristics curve will specify the nature of the charge transport mechanism. The variations in the shapes and sizes of the nanostructures are responsible for the changes in the barrier height, often attributed to the quantum confinement effect, which severely alters their optical and electrical properties [19,65].

Fig. 6 (a, b) and Fig. S5 show the I–V characteristic spectra of doped and un-doped CDs in the absence and presence of a redox-active molecule MD in both H2O–EtOH and micro-emulsion media. However, our main aim is to investigate the extent of electron transport on the doping of CDs and observe the effect of conductance by changing their surrounding environment. The spectroscopic study infers an exciting result that the heterogeneous medium has several charged groups at their surface/periphery, which decide the nature of interaction with the specific nanodot when they are close by. This mode of interaction can be identified by tracing out their current-voltage spectroscopy. The nature of the I–V spectrum of UCD and ACD displays a nonlinear or exponential, as shown in Fig. 6 (a, b). However, they show a linear increase in current with the applied voltage at the lower bias region while a sharp increase in current with higher applied voltage. Irrespective of the nature of the surrounding environment, as is shown in the above plots, the UCD is observed to be less conducting compared to ACD. However, the extent of enhancement in the conductivity is very significant for H2O–EtOH one compared to the confined medium. This is probably due to higher carrier concentration in the ACDs compared to UCDs, as observed in the fluorescence intensity as well as lifetime measurements, which may lead to the enhancement in the conductivity [29]. Also, due to greater conjugation in the amides compared to the carboxylic acids and due to the quantum interference effect, the conductivity of the system having amides bonds will be higher than the carboxylic acids [66,67]. On the other hand, the strong electronic coupling between lone pair of amine with the electrode of the AFM tip, moreover the electron-donating group attached to a system will give the better enhancement in the conductivity of the system [68]. Several reports suggest that doping increases the density of state and decreases the bandgap of the material, creating a trap state which enhances the conductivity [69,70]. However, a certain question is arising and also creating confusion that, why the conductance of any types of nano-dots system is enhanced while treating it with the CTAB medium? To answer this, Osorio et al. [71] suggest that ionic liquids and/or electrolytes are effective medium for the conductance at the single molecular level compared to the H2O–EtOH medium as the electrochemical gate coupling is more active [72,73]. It gives a platform to make a bridge between the molecule and electrode for the transport of charge carriers, but in solid-state device or thin-film case, this scenario is achieved by bringing the gate terminal in close proximity to the molecule. Hence, the enhancement of the current in the output electrode with applied bias voltage will be observed in the confined medium (CTAB), which can also be treated as ionic liquid [71]. Several theoretical and experimental studies suggest that there are various factors responsible for junction conductance across a semiconductor like size, DOS, local electron concentration, molecular HOMO - LUMO energy gap, molecular bonding, functional architecture, and contact properties etc. [74–77]. All these factors are calculated from their current (I) vs. voltage (V) characteristic graphs. The charge transport mechanism observed through the I–V measurement of the system is based on quantum tunneling. In molecular electronics, nanoscale-based circuit elements are widespread to provide asymmetric/nonlinear shapes in their current-voltage behavior. According to this model, when applied bias is lower than the barrier height, a direct rectangular barrier is observed while at higher bias exceed the barrier height, the electron is pass through a triangular barrier [78,79]. Hence, the barrier height, which is the key factor at the metal-semiconductor-metal junction, is calculated using the following modified Simmons’ Equation (7) (given in experimental section) [80,81]. Fig. 6 (a, b) gives the idea of the impact of MD on the I–V curves of both the CDs when the sample is prepared in the H2O–EtOH mixture. The addition of MD not only enhances the conductivity of the system sharply but also changes the nature of tunneling from FN to direct or Ohmic one. Such type of enhancement in conductivity by the inclusion of MD is also observed when the solution is in CTAB medium, as reflected in Fig. 6 (b). Though the reason behind the transport pathway is quite simple, but its reaction mechanism is a little complicated. The literature report suggests that due to cross conjugation, there will be destructive interference among the various conductance channels in the quinones, which is missing in linear conjugated hydroquinol forms. Thus, the conductance of the hydroquinol (reduced) will be higher than its quinone (oxidation) form. So, in the quinone-hydroquinol system, an increase in ET to the quinone system leads to the reduction of the quinone to the quinol and hence a further increase in the conductance value [27,82–84]. Moreover, through hydrogen bonding, Coulombic interaction, and/or other interactions with the solvent molecule, intermediate new charge states generated through electron acceptance (and hence change in oxidation state) from the parent quinone molecule may take place. We hypothesize this is probably the reason behind the enhancement of conductivity with the addition of quinone-MD.

To understand the intrinsic mechanism behind it, we have tried to fit the low bias region of the I–V curve (Fig. 6 (a, b)) by the direct tunneling Equation (7) due to its Ohmic nature, which fit well and the higher bias voltage region is fitted by F–N equation by using Simmons’ Equation (8). At low bias conditions, the low energy electron can’t pass through the broad rectangular barrier. Hence, the current, detected as leakage current, occurs due to small emission current/tunneling current due to direct diffusion of electron [85]. With the increase in bias voltage, the barrier width decreases by forming a triangular barrier. Gradually, when the voltage reaches to transition voltage, the electron having enough energy, finally overcome the narrowed barrier by FN-tunneling. So, the linear curve is converted to an exponential with the increase in bias voltage. Moreover, FN tunneling is the prime influence at high bias/high current leads to large emission current. Therefore, depending upon the barrier width, the electron can tunnel with signatures of both linear and exponential dependence in the I–V curves. The resistances of the fitted curve for UCD and ACD in the absence of MD are calculated from their slope of the linear portion (green colour in Fig. 6 (a, b)) at the lower bias voltage. For the case of UCDs, it is interesting to note that the values are decreasing from 0.28 to 0.14 GΩ from H2O–EtOH to CTAB medium (Table 1). However, there is a minimal change in its value for ACD (0.13 GΩ), which correlates well with our proposed hypothesis of electrostatic interaction at the interface. Similarly, the addition of MD in these CDs also decreases the resistance with enhancing the conductivity in both the media. It is apparent from the resistance calculation that even at a higher concentration of MD, these systems offer certain minimum resistance during measurement, which is independent of the nature of the medium. Based on their results Ratner et al. have found a relation between the ET rate in the D-bridge-A system with the conductance of the same bridge in the metal-molecule-metal junction as mentioned in the experimental section as Equation (9) [86]. To calibrate the relation between ET and electron transport (ETp), we have calculated the change in resistance (details given in the Experimental Section) and compare it with the ET rate. Fig. 6 (c) shows the comparison data of the ET rate with the change in the resistance value of individual CDs in both media. It shows that there is a one to one relation between them, which means the system having a high transfer rate gives lower resistance or higher conductance and vice-versa. This gives a straight-forward indication of the ET and ETp relation in this quantum dot system.

Using various spectroscopic and AFM techniques, we, in this article, have investigated the role of doping as well as the nature of interfaces for the dynamics of charge transfer and transport in CDs. Photo-induced charge transfer from amine doped ACDs and undoped UCDs to redox-active biomolecule MD are observed to be disrupted in H2O–EtOH binary mixture and CTAB micro-emulsion. The nitrogen-doped ACDs shows a higher radiative lifetime compared to UCDs. However, in comparison with ACDs, a higher rate of charge transfer is reported for UCDs in the micellar media, resulting from Columbic interaction between the CDs and the cationic CTAB interface. The negatively charged UCD faces electrostatic attraction at the positive interface of CTAB, facilitating the ET from UCDs to the encapsulated MD, while the positively charged ACD faces a repulsion restricting the ET rates. Conductivity across the electrode-CD-electrode junction is also observed to be influenced by doping and surface charges. An increased charge transport across ACDs is observed owing to the increased interference effect present in the conjugated amides in ACDs compared to the carboxylic acids in UCDs. The addition of MD to both the CDs enhances the conductivity sharply and changes the nature of tunneling from FN to direct or Ohmic type. Such investigation of charge transfers as well as charge transport in CDs can open up new approaches in their optoelectronic applications.

Leepsa Mishra: Conceptualization, execution, design, Spectroscopic characterizations, Interpretation, Writing – original draft. Ranjan Kumar Behera: Conceptualization, design, measurement, CS-AFM analysis, Writing – original draft. Sankalan Mondal: Synthesis, Schematic artwork, spectral analysis, Writing – original draft. Satish Kumar: Writing – original draft, discussion. Aradhana Panigrahi: Writing – original draft, discussion.
